lineitem.notes: Largest table, always filtered by date ranges. Multiple computed columns (price*(1-discount)*(1+tax)) suggest expression evaluation optimization opportunities.

orders.notes: Joined via PK (o_orderkey) and FK (o_custkey). Date range filter in Q3.

customer.notes: Small dimension table. c_mktsegment has low cardinality (~5 values) - highly selective filter.

nation.notes: Tiny dimension table, not accessed in test queries but present in schema.

region.notes: Tiny dimension table, not accessed in test queries.

partsupp.notes: Many-to-many relationship table, not accessed in test queries.

joins.notes: Q3: Should execute AFTER filtering customer by c_mktsegment='BUILDING'. Filter reduces customer from 150K to ~30K rows (1/5 selectivity).
Q3: Should execute AFTER filtering orders by o_orderdate and lineitem by l_shipdate. Each order has ~4 lineitems on average.

filters.notes: Date filter: l_shipdate <= '1998-09-02'. Filters to ~98% of data (high selectivity = most rows pass). Zone maps critical.
1-year range: '1994-01-01' to '1994-12-31'. Filters to ~14% of data (1 year / 7 years). Zone maps very effective.
Range: 0.05 to 0.07 (2% width out of typical 0-10% range). Selectivity ~20%.
Point filter: 'BUILDING'. Low cardinality column (5 values), so ~20% selectivity. Prime candidate for dictionary encoding.
o_orderdate < '1995-03-15'. Filters to orders before mid-1995, roughly 30-40% of data.
l_shipdate > '1995-03-15'. Complementary to orders filter - creates a temporal window.

aggregations.notes: Very low cardinality GROUP BY (returnflag: 3 values {A,N,R}, linestatus: 2 values {O,F} = 4-6 groups). Hash aggregation overkill - sorted aggregation with single-pass scan is optimal. Multiple aggregates (6 SUMs, 3 AVGs) suggest vectorized evaluation.

Medium-high cardinality GROUP BY. l_orderkey is unique per group. Hash aggregation appropriate. Top-K optimization possible: maintain min-heap of size 10 during aggregation instead of full hash table.

ordering.notes: Sorting 4-6 groups is trivial. Can avoid sort if aggregation produces results in sorted order (e.g., sorted aggregation or small hash table with post-sort).

Top-10 query with ORDER BY + LIMIT. Classic optimization opportunity: use min-heap during aggregation to track top 10, avoiding full sort of 50K-100K groups. Revenue is computed column, needs materialization.

optimization_opportunities: Zone maps on date columns (l_shipdate, o_orderdate): TPC-H dates span 1992-1998. All queries have date filters. Zone maps can skip 80-90% of blocks.
Columnar storage for lineitem: All queries scan lineitem but access only 5-8 of 16 columns. Columnar layout reduces I/O by 50-70%.
Dictionary encoding for low-cardinality strings: c_mktsegment (5 values), l_returnflag (3 values), l_linestatus (2 values). Reduces memory footprint and enables integer comparisons.

Filter pushdown in Q3: Apply c_mktsegment filter first (reduces customer 5x), then join to orders. Current order matters: customer (30K) -> orders (150K) -> lineitem (600K) produces smaller intermediates than lineitem (6M) -> orders -> customer.

Join ordering for Q3: Optimal order is customer |><| orders |><| lineitem. Greedy heuristic: filter customer (150K -> 30K), join with filtered orders (1.5M -> 500K filtered by date), then join with filtered lineitem.
Computed expressions in Q1 and Q3: l_extendedprice * (1 - l_discount) appears in Q1, Q3, Q6. Consider pre-computing or using SIMD FMA (fused multiply-add) instructions.
Top-K optimization for Q3: Maintain heap of size 10 during aggregation instead of sorting 50K-100K groups. Reduces sort from O(n log n) to O(n log k) where k=10.
Sorted aggregation for Q1: Only 4-6 groups. If lineitem is sorted by (l_returnflag, l_linestatus) or scan produces sorted output, use single-pass sorted aggregation (O(1) memory vs hash table).
Late materialization in Q3: After joins and filters, only need l_orderkey for GROUP BY and 3 columns for final output. Avoid materializing full lineitem rows during join pipeline.
Vectorized execution for Q6: Tight loop with 4 filters (shipdate range, discount range, quantity threshold, expression computation). Ideal for SIMD: process 8 rows at once with AVX2.
Correlated column storage: (l_extendedprice, l_discount, l_tax) always accessed together for revenue computation. Store as column group for better cache locality.
Bloom filters for semi-join in Q3: Build bloom filter on filtered customer.c_custkey, probe during orders scan to skip non-matching orders early.
summary: This workload is dominated by lineitem scans with selective date filters (Q1, Q6) and a 3-way join with temporal filters (Q3). Key characteristics: (1) Lineitem is the bottleneck - largest table, accessed in all queries. (2) Date filters are highly selective and clustered - zone maps will be extremely effective. (3) Low-cardinality aggregations (Q1: 4 groups) and Top-K pattern (Q3: LIMIT 10) offer specialized optimization opportunities. (4) Computed expressions (price*(1-discount)*(1+tax)) appear repeatedly - SIMD or pre-computation worthwhile. (5) Q3 join ordering matters: filter customer first (20% selectivity), then cascade to orders and lineitem. Recommended optimizations: columnar storage + zone maps for lineitem, dictionary encoding for flags/segments, filter pushdown + optimal join ordering for Q3, top-K heap for Q3, vectorized execution for expression-heavy Q1/Q6.
